lens.included_activities: Lower-middle-market-scale political parties, political action committees, and multi-cycle political organizations with recurring fundraising, compliance, voter contact, communications, field, and administrative operations serving a party, candidates, donors, or supporters.
lens.excluded_activities: One-cycle candidate committees without a recurring operating organization, dormant or winding-down committees, social-advocacy nonprofits, lobbying and public-relations agencies, contract fundraising and telemarketing vendors, government offices, shells, and non-control financing vehicles.
lens.customer_and_revenue_model: Organizations raise regulated contributions and transfers and spend them on candidate or party support, voter contact, media, fundraising, field operations, compliance, staff, and administration; supporters and political principals fund mission activity rather than purchasing a conventional fee-for-service product.
lens.deviation_from_default: NAICS 813940 mixes durable party and multi-cycle committee operations with temporary single-candidate campaign entities. The lens retains recurring organizations and excludes one-cycle or winding-down committees because combining both operating lives would make five-year implementation, demand, and transfer assumptions incoherent.

a.bridge: Source population and quantity: BLS May 2023 OEWS for the broader NAICS 813900 group reports 20.84% office and administrative support, 19.84% management, 28.07% business and financial operations, and 3.59% public-relations specialists; O*NET lists public-relations work including media response, website and social content, press releases, publications, research, promotional planning, and relationship management. Transformation: the broader occupation mix and PR tasks are mapped to recurring political organizations, assigning high exposure to drafting, donor and voter segmentation, research, monitoring, translation, compliance preparation, scheduling, and content variants, but low exposure to political judgment, persuasion, coalition relationships, field leadership, approvals, and accountable decisions. Direction of error: the industry group is dominated by nonpolitical member organizations and may misstate the political occupation mix; campaign work may be more digital and exposed, while high-stakes persuasion, originality, authenticity, and field work may make effective substitution lower.
a.rationale: Political operations are unusually information- and communications-intensive, but their outcomes depend on strategy, trust, candidate voice, rapid contextual judgment, field execution, legal approval, and human relationships.
a.change_evidence: A wage-linked time study across parties, PACs, and multi-cycle committees covering fundraising, media, digital, research, compliance, finance, field, data, and administration, with current vendor automation identified, would materially change this range.
caveats: The available OEWS mix covers the broader 813900 group rather than 813940 and is not a wage-weighted task-time measure.
Public-relations tasks omit campaign-specific field, compliance, fundraising, data, creative, and political-strategy roles.
Political organizations already use extensive digital tools and vendors, so nominal task exposure may include work that is already automated.

rho.bridge: Source population and quantity: Census BTOS data show overall U.S. business AI use between 17% and 20% from December 2025 to May 2026 and expected six-month use between 20% and 23%. Transformation: those national rates are projected over five years for digitally intensive political organizations, allowing faster uptake in communications, research, analytics, translation, administration, and compliance support but discounting election deadlines, model error, security, donor and voter privacy, platform rules, campaign-finance law, approval chains, reputational risk, and staff turnover. Direction of error: business-wide any-function adoption may overstate reliable workflow integration, while political organizations' strong incentives for speed and experimentation may produce faster adoption than the national average.
rho.rationale: Many exposed tasks can be layered onto existing campaign, CRM, digital, finance, and content systems, but sensitive data, legal rules, adversarial scrutiny, synthetic-content risks, and short campaign cycles require strong human control.
rho.change_evidence: A panel of parties, PACs, and recurring committees measuring active AI users, covered workflows, human-review rates, production time, fundraising lift, field productivity, compliance exceptions, security incidents, vendor costs, and avoided hiring would materially change this estimate.
caveats: BTOS does not identify political organizations or election-cycle deployment.
Using AI in any business function is not equivalent to operationally implementing the exposed opportunity.
High staff turnover and decentralized campaigns can both accelerate experimentation and prevent durable implementation.

e.rationale: Census includes parties, PACs, campaign organizations, and fundraising groups, while IRS and FEC describe entities organized to accept contributions and make political expenditures under regulated party or committee structures. The recurring-service and commercial-size lens fits only a small subset: many committees are candidate-specific, temporary, volunteer-led, legally controlled by political principals, or lack transferable normalized EBITDA even when receipts fall in the nominal band.
e.change_evidence: A committee-level census combining FEC and state registrations, filing continuity across cycles, payroll, vendor concentration, recurring staff and systems, receipts, unrestricted operating resources, candidate or party control, and termination status would materially change the eligible share.
caveats: The frozen n estimate applies a commercial margin bridge to contribution-funded entities for which normalized EBITDA is not an economic ownership measure.
Federal filings omit state and local organizations, while Census establishments may include entities outside FEC jurisdiction.
Narrowing to recurring organizations excludes a large but operationally temporary part of the NAICS definition.

s5.rationale: Political committees and parties are governed by candidates, party structures, law, bylaws, and political constituencies rather than salable equity. Candidate succession, elections, officer changes, affiliations, and committee termination are not qualifying control transfers; only rare combinations or asset-and-operation successions could resemble one.
s5.change_evidence: Five years of FEC and state records on committee mergers, affiliations, asset transfers, successor committees, terminations, and control changes matched to the recurring eligible population would establish whether any events meet a qualifying-control definition.
caveats: No source measures qualifying control transfers among political organizations.
A new candidate, chair, treasurer, or election result does not itself transfer a salable operating business.
Committee assets and donor relationships are legally and politically constrained and may not be transferable like commercial goodwill.

q.rationale: Contributions and political transfers are not cost-plus customer prices that automatically fall when workflows become cheaper. Most implemented benefit can remain as additional voter contact, fundraising, research, compliance capacity, or cash preservation, although software and data vendors, platform costs, consultant fees, donor expectations, and expanded activity absorb some benefit.
q.change_evidence: Committee-level before-and-after data on vendor spend, payroll, content throughput, donor acquisition cost, fundraising net revenue, compliance cost, field contacts, media purchasing, and cash retained per dollar raised would materially change retention.
caveats: No public source measures retained AI benefit in political organizations.
Savings are likely redeployed into competitive activity rather than realized as distributable profit.
Vendor and platform pricing may capture a meaningful share of productivity gains.

d5.bridge: Source population and quantity: FEC reports that in the 2023-2024 federal cycle presidential candidates raised $2.0 billion, congressional candidates $3.8 billion, parties $2.7 billion, and PACs $15.7 billion; its historical table shows congressional receipts of $3.8 billion in 2024 versus $4.0 billion in 2020 and $2.8 billion in 2018. Transformation: recurring federal campaign-finance activity and its large cycle-to-cycle variation are converted into a five-year constant-price demand ratio for the current political-organization service basket, with a wide range for election calendar, competitiveness, regulation, donor response, media prices, state activity, and real fundraising. Direction of error: nominal federal receipts overstate real service-volume growth and include transfers and entities outside the narrowed lens, while excluding state and local activity and volunteer effort may understate demand.
d5.rationale: Elections recur and large fundraising and spending volumes sustain political operations, but demand is highly cyclical, concentrated in competitive races, sensitive to regulation and donors, and difficult to separate from media-price inflation and transfers.
d5.change_evidence: Inflation-adjusted FEC and state campaign-finance panels by recurring committee, combined with staff hours, voter contacts, donor transactions, communications volume, and election competitiveness across comparable cycles, would materially change this range.
caveats: FEC receipts and disbursements are nominal financial flows, not constant-price, constant-quality service quantity.
Federal totals include temporary candidate committees and transfers that fall outside the narrowed lens.
The five-year endpoint can fall in a different phase of the two- and four-year election cycles.

o.bridge: Source population and quantity: FEC requires qualifying party committees to have formal structures, candidates, day-to-day operations, regulated funds, and regular reporting; IRS defines section 527 political organizations around accepting contributions and making expenditures for exempt political functions. Transformation: most year-five political activity is treated as still requiring an accountable committee, party, candidate organization, treasurer, and human political leadership even when AI generates content, performs research, segments audiences, or automates administration. Direction of error: legal accountability and political legitimacy support a high operator-required share, but self-service campaign platforms, candidate-controlled digital operations, centralized parties, and AI agents could displace more standalone organization activity than assumed.
o.rationale: Software can automate execution but cannot itself be the candidate, hold party legitimacy, lawfully govern a committee, authorize regulated money, bear filing responsibility, or maintain the coalition and public accountability behind political decisions.
o.change_evidence: FEC and state evidence on committees operating with minimal staff, self-service campaign platforms, centralized compliance and fundraising, AI-generated communications, treasurer workload, and which functions legally and practically require a distinct organization would materially change this share.
caveats: The sources establish legal organizational roles but do not measure a five-year operator-required quantity share.
A party, candidate, or platform can internalize functions while political activity continues without a separate LMM-scale organization.
AI may sharply reduce staffing and vendors even where a formal committee remains legally necessary.

narrative.executive_view: Political organizations have unusually high exposure in content, research, fundraising, data, compliance preparation, and administration, but the industry mixes durable parties and PACs with temporary campaigns. AI can raise campaign capacity, while regulated governance, cyclical demand, contribution-funded economics, and the absence of salable ownership weaken a roll-up thesis.
narrative.ai_impact: AI can draft and adapt messages, summarize issues and opposition research, segment donors and voters, translate content, prepare compliance work, monitor media, support fundraising, and automate scheduling and administration. Human leaders remain responsible for strategy, candidate voice, legal approval, coalition relationships, field leadership, and reputational judgment.
narrative.value_capture: Because funding arrives as contributions rather than cost-plus customer prices, productivity can remain as additional political capacity. Competitive pressure will usually redeploy gains into more outreach, fundraising, media, or field activity, while vendors and platforms capture part of the benefit.
narrative.firm_availability: A small set of durable parties, PACs, and multi-cycle committees has recurring staff and systems, but most entities are temporary, principal-controlled, volunteer-heavy, or legally constrained rather than transferable owner-controlled firms. Commercial EBITDA and acquisition concepts fit poorly.
narrative.demand_durability: Recurring elections and large regulated financial flows sustain demand, but the calendar, race competitiveness, donor sentiment, law, platform rules, and media prices create large cycle-to-cycle variation that obscures constant-quality volume.
narrative.risks_and_uncertainty: The largest uncertainties are the true 813940 occupation mix, existing digital automation, synthetic-content backlash, privacy and cybersecurity, election law, temporary-entity prevalence, state activity, real versus nominal demand, and the commercial margin bridge behind the frozen firm count.
narrative.principal_driver: High-volume digital communications, fundraising, research, data, and compliance workflows create substantial AI-assisted operating leverage.
narrative.principal_weakness: Temporary campaign lives, regulated political control, contribution-funded economics, and almost nonexistent salable ownership break the conventional transfer mechanism.

sources.title: NAICS Sector 81 Definitions: Political Organizations
sources.supports: Defines 813940 as organizations promoting parties or candidates and includes political campaigns, PACs, clubs, parties, and fundraising groups while excluding contract fundraising and telemarketing vendors.

sources.title: BLS May 2023 OEWS: Business, Professional, Labor, Political, and Similar Organizations
sources.supports: Reports the broader 813900 occupation mix, including 20.84% office support, 19.84% management, 28.07% business and financial operations, and 3.59% public-relations specialists.

sources.title: O*NET OnLine: Public Relations Specialists
sources.supports: Lists media response, website and social content, press releases, publications, research, promotional planning, and relationship-management tasks.

sources.title: Census Bureau: Large Firms With at Least 20 Employees Biggest AI Users
sources.supports: Reports overall U.S. business AI use of 17%-20% from December 2025 to May 2026 and expected six-month use of 20%-23%.

sources.title: FEC: Statistical Summary of 24-Month Campaign Activity of the 2023-2024 Election Cycle
sources.supports: Reports 2023-2024 federal candidate, party, and PAC receipts and disbursements and historical congressional-candidate activity across election cycles.

sources.title: FEC: Qualifying as a Political Party Committee
sources.supports: Describes formal party structures, ballot and candidate criteria, day-to-day operations, regulated funds, and regular reporting obligations for political party committees.

sources.title: IRS: Exempt Organization Types
sources.supports: Defines a section 527 political organization as a party, committee, association, fund, or other organization operated primarily to accept contributions or make expenditures for an exempt political function.
